content.text: The service catalog creates value only when teams know how to find and consume the information it holds. A catalog with accurate metadata but no standardised consumption patterns becomes a lookup tool that individuals use sporadically, rather than the queryable platform layer that drives automated decisions. This sub-subtopic covers the six canonical access channels, the REST API query patterns for finding GenAI components, the platform Python SDK, programmatic dependency registration, and platform-level dashboards built on catalog data.
Search for existing RAG or agent components before building a new one
Scripted checks, local validation, ad-hoc catalog queries
Services querying the catalog at build time or runtime
A service bootstrap script resolves its downstream dependencies from the catalog
Teams creating new services via Backstage templates
A new service template pre-populates its spec.dependsOn by querying existing embedding services
Runs catalog-info.yaml linting and scorecard validation on every PR
Cross-service cost attribution, model inventory reporting, data-class risk register
Backstage UI: Faceted Search for GenAI Components
The Backstage Explore and Catalog pages expose a filter panel. The platform team configures GenAI-specific filter facets by extending the Backstage frontend with a custom
Data class: public | internal | confidential | restricted
Golden path: rag | agent | gateway | none

Teams use the search to check whether an embedding service for their domain already exists before spinning up a new one. The component page shows the full annotation set, the TechDocs link, the eval suite dashboard, and the real-time scorecard badge.
The Backstage catalog exposes a REST API at
parameter accepts comma-separated key=value pairs that are AND-ed together; multiple values for the same key are OR-ed.

BACKSTAGE="https://backstage.internal/api/catalog"
TOKEN="Bearer $BACKSTAGE_TOKEN"

# Find all production RAG applications
curl -s "$BACKSTAGE/entities?kind=Component&filter=metadata.annotations.genai.io/type=rag-application,spec.lifecycle=production" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" | jq '.[] | {name:.metadata.name, model:.metadata.annotations["genai.io/primary-model"]}'

# Find all components using a premium-tier model that are NOT yet in production
curl -s "$BACKSTAGE/entities?kind=Component&filter=metadata.annotations.genai.io/model-tier=premium,spec.lifecycle=experimental" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" | jq '.[].metadata.name'

# Find all components that handle restricted data
curl -s "$BACKSTAGE/entities?kind=Component&filter=metadata.annotations.genai.io/data-class=restricted" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" | jq '.[] | {name:.metadata.name, owner:.spec.owner}'

# Fetch a single component by name (exact lookup)
curl -s "$BACKSTAGE/entities/by-name/component/default/product-search-rag" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" | jq '.metadata.annotations'
query parameter to limit the response payload —
— especially when querying all components for a nightly report. Without projection, each entity response can be several kilobytes.
package that wraps the REST API with typed methods. It resolves the Backstage token from the credential resolver automatically.

from genai_auth.catalog import CatalogClient, ComponentFilter

client = CatalogClient()   # reads BACKSTAGE_URL and BACKSTAGE_TOKEN from env

# Find the production embedding service for a given domain
def find_embedding_service(domain_tag: str) -> dict | None:
    results = client.find_components(
        ComponentFilter(
            genai_type="embedding-service",
            lifecycle="production",
            tags=[domain_tag],
        )
    )
    # Return the first result sorted by scorecard score (highest first)
    return max(results, key=lambda c: c.get("scorecard_score", 0), default=None)

# Get a specific component's full annotation set
def get_component_metadata(name: str) -> dict:
    return client.get_component(name)["metadata"]["annotations"]

# Register a dependency relationship programmatically (used during onboarding)
def register_dependency(component_name: str, depends_on: str) -> None:
    """
    Opens a PR to the component's repo adding depends_on to spec.dependsOn.
    This is a platform convenience wrapper around the GitHub API.
    In most cases teams set spec.dependsOn manually in catalog-info.yaml.
    """
    client.add_dependency(component_name, f"component:default/{depends_on}")
Platform-Level Dashboards Built on Catalog Data
The nightly analytics pipeline joins catalog metadata with operational signals from Prometheus and the LLM gateway to produce dashboards that no individual service can produce alone.
catalog (golden-path annotation) vs total component count

The following walkthrough shows how a team building a new customer-support agent discovers an existing embedding service in the catalog, registers the dependency, and wires the resolved URL into their service startup.
Search the catalog for a production embedding service tagged with the support domain.
Review the component page: check data-class, model tier, eval suite score, and TechDocs for the API contract.
Add the component to spec.dependsOn in the new service's catalog-info.yaml.
Resolve the base URL at service startup using CatalogClient and cache it.
Point the LLM SDK's EmbeddingAdapter at the resolved URL.
Verify the dependency appears in the Backstage dependency graph view before opening the production gate PR.

# support_agent/startup.py
import os
from genai_auth.catalog import CatalogClient, ComponentFilter
from internal_llm_sdk.adapters.embedding import RemoteEmbeddingAdapter
from internal_llm_sdk.client import LLMClient
from genai_telemetry import bootstrap

bootstrap(
    service_name    = "support-agent",
    service_version = os.environ["APP_VERSION"],
    deployment_env  = os.environ.get("DEPLOYMENT_ENV", "development"),
)

# ── Resolve embedding service URL from catalog ─────────────────────────────
_catalog = CatalogClient()
_embed_svc: dict | None = None

def get_embedding_url() -> str:
    global _embed_svc
    if _embed_svc is None:
        results = _catalog.find_components(
            ComponentFilter(genai_type="embedding-service",
                            lifecycle="production",
                            tags=["support"])
        )
        if not results:
            raise RuntimeError("No production embedding service tagged 'support' found in catalog")
        _embed_svc = results[0]
    annots = _embed_svc["metadata"]["annotations"]
    base_url = annots.get("genai.io/service-url")
    if not base_url:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Embedding service {_embed_svc['metadata']['name']} has no genai.io/service-url annotation"
        )
    return base_url

# ── Build LLM client using resolved embedding service ─────────────────────
def build_agent_client() -> LLMClient:
    embed_url = get_embedding_url()
    embed_adapter = RemoteEmbeddingAdapter(base_url=embed_url)
    # ... build full agent with embed_adapter
    return embed_adapter

annotation is an optional but strongly recommended addition to every production component. It allows consumers to resolve the service endpoint from the catalog without hard-coding it or maintaining a separate service-discovery config file.

The Backstage catalog emits webhook events when entity state changes. The platform team configures a webhook forwarder that pushes these events to an internal Kafka topic. Services that need to react to catalog changes — for example, an API gateway that updates its routing table when a new LLM service is registered — subscribe to this topic rather than polling the REST API.

# catalog_event_consumer.py — example consumer for catalog change events
from aiokafka import AIOKafkaConsumer
import json, asyncio

CATALOG_TOPIC = "platform.catalog.entity-changes"

async def handle_catalog_event(event: dict) -> None:
    entity_type = event.get("entityRef", "").split("/")[0].lower()
    event_type  = event.get("type")  # "CREATED" | "UPDATED" | "DELETED"

    if entity_type != "component":
        return
    if event_type == "CREATED":
        name   = event["entity"]["metadata"]["name"]
        annots = event["entity"]["metadata"].get("annotations", {})
        genai_type = annots.get("genai.io/type")
        if genai_type == "llm-gateway":
            # New LLM gateway registered — update the gateway routing table
            await refresh_gateway_routes()
    elif event_type == "UPDATED":
        lifecycle = event["entity"]["spec"].get("lifecycle")
        if lifecycle == "deprecated":
            # Send deprecation notice to consumers
            await notify_consumers(event["entity"]["metadata"]["name"])

async def consume():
    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer(
        CATALOG_TOPIC,
        bootstrap_servers="kafka.internal:9092",
        group_id="support-agent-catalog-watcher",
        value_deserializer=lambda v: json.loads(v),
    )
    await consumer.start()
    async for msg in consumer:
        await handle_catalog_event(msg.value)
Hardcoding a service URL instead of resolving from catalog
Service URL changes require touching every consumer; no central visibility
Resolve the URL from catalog at service startup via CatalogClient.get_component()
Using the Backstage UI only (no API integration)
Platform dashboards and CI checks cannot consume catalog data automatically
Treat the REST API as the primary interface; the UI is for human browsing
Querying catalog at runtime on every request
Adds ~50ms latency to every LLM call; catalog is not a real-time store
Cache catalog lookups at startup and refresh every 5 minutes
Not registering spec.dependsOn after consuming a service
Dependency graph is incomplete; cascade alerts miss your team; EOL impact analysis is wrong
Add spec.dependsOn to catalog-info.yaml before going to production
Bypassing the catalog to find services via Slack/wiki
Ad-hoc discovery misses deprecated services; no guarantee of current metadata
Always use the Backstage search or CatalogClient as the first step

Catalog discovery and consumption patterns determine whether the investment in metadata pays off. Six access channels — the Backstage UI, CLI, platform Python SDK, scaffolder templates, CI workflows, and analytics dashboards — each serve a different audience and automation context. The REST API's filter-and-fields query model supports everything from point lookups to cross-service inventory reports. The
Python wrapper makes programmatic discovery a first-class operation in service startup scripts and analytics pipelines. Registering dependencies in

closes the loop: it makes the dependency graph visible to cascade alerting, impact analysis, and EOL decommission checks. Teams that consume the catalog through its standard interfaces gain a reliable, governance-enforced view of the GenAI landscape without maintaining their own spreadsheets or wikis.
